---
title: "L01 Review"
subtitle: "Data Science 3 with R (STAT 301-3)"
author: "Nick Hanna"
pagetitle: "L01 Nick Hanna"
date: today

format:
  html:
    theme: cosmo
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 4
    toc-location: left
    embed-resources: true
    code-fold: false
    link-external-newwindow: true

execute:
  warning: false

from: markdown+emoji
reference-location: margin
citation-location: margin   
---

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Github Repo Link

To link to your github **repo**sitory, appropriately edit the example link below. Meaning replace `https://your-github-repo-url` with your github repo url. Suggest verifying the link works before submitting.

[nickhanna2026](https://github.com/stat301-3-2026-spring/l01-review-nickhanna2026)
:::

## Overview

The goal of this lab is to review machine learning/predictive modeling vocabulary and concepts covered in Data Science 2 with R (STAT 301-2).

## Questions

When completing the following questions ensure your solutions are neatly formatted and clearly indicated. Consider including diagrams/images in some of your solutions, if it helps to make things easier to describe/discuss.

### Question 1

Provide a brief outline/overview of the steps involved in a supervised machine learning process. Could provide this as a bulleted list. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

1. Come up with a predictive or other type of question to answer. If the question is predictive, determine whether the outcome variable renders the model regression or classification.  
2. Gather data and then split into training and testing which is reserved for assessing the accuracy of your model 
3. Explore the data set, this can include exploring the total data set and the training data set, one should not explore the testing data set. 
4. Fold and resample the training data to create more partitions of the data to improve the models generalizability and reduce overfitting. 
5. Feature engineer and create recipes for the folded data based on the EDA to improve and facilitate the models performance before creating a workflows for the models. Prep and bake the recipes into training data. Use both basic and more complex recipes. 
6. Define and fit simpler models and a null model to create baseline. 
7. Define more complex models, workflows, and tune relevant hyperparameters to create multiple model types that have combinations of various hyperparameters to see which performs best on the folded data. 
8. Assess the best model types and hyperparameter combinations and then choose one distinct workflow and model type (the best performing one) to test on the testing data. 
9. Assess the best model on the testing data and review results/come up with takeaways 


:::

### Question 2

Explain the difference between supervised and unsupervised learning.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Supervised learning uses labeled data, meaning each observation has a known outcome, and the model learns to predict that outcome (like predicting default or not). Unsupervised learning, on the other hand, works with unlabeled data and tries to find patterns or structure on its own, like grouping similar observations together. Basically, supervised learning is about prediction with known answers, while unsupervised learning is about discovering hidden patterns without predefined labels.
:::

### Question 3 

In general, we can classify a model by its purpose into 1 of the 3 categories below. Provide a brief description of the goals of these model classes.

#### Descriptive Models 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Inferential Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

#### Predictive Models

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 4 

We can further describe/classify predictive models by how they were derived or developed as being either mechanistic or empirically driven. 

#### Part (a)

What does it mean to be a mechanistic model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

A mechanistic model is one that is built based on how a system actually works, using known relationships or underlying processes. Instead of just fitting patterns in the data, it tries to represent cause-and-effect. So the focus is on explaining why something happens, not just predicting what will happen.
:::

#### Part (b)

What does it mean to be an empirically driven model?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

An empirically driven model is built mainly from data rather than from underlying theory or known mechanisms. It focuses on finding patterns and relationships in the data that help make accurate predictions, even if we don’t fully understand the cause behind them. So instead of explaining why something happens, the goal is to capture what tends to happen based on observed data.
:::

#### Part (c)

How does the mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the parametric and nonparametric model terminology? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

They’re related ideas, but not exactly the same thing. Mechanistic models are often parametric because they’re based on a specific assumed form (like an equation from theory with a fixed number of parameters). Empirically driven models are more often nonparametric since they’re flexible and let the data determine the shape of the relationship. That said, it’s not a perfect one-to-one match. You can have empirically driven parametric models (like linear regression) and even some structured nonparametric models, so the terms overlap but focus on different aspects.
:::

#### Part (d)

In general, is a mechanistic or empirically driven model easier to interpret? Explain.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

In general, mechanistic models are easier to interpret because they are built around known relationships and clear cause-and-effect assumptions. Each parameter usually has a direct meaning, so you can explain how changes in inputs affect the outcome. Empirically driven models, especially more flexible ones, can be harder to interpret because they focus on capturing patterns in the data rather than explaining why those patterns exist.
:::

#### Part (e)

How does mechanistic and empirically driven model terminology relate to the idea of model flexibility? That is, which would be more or less flexible than the other.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Mechanistic models are usually less flexible because they follow a specific, theory-driven structure, so the form of the model is more fixed. Empirically driven models are generally more flexible since they let the data determine the relationship and can adapt to more complex patterns.So in general, mechanistic means less flexible, empirically driven means more flexible.
:::

#### Part (f)

Describe the bias-variance trade-off when considering the use of a mechanistic or empirically driven model. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Mechanistic models tend to have higher bias and lower variance because they assume a fixed structure that might not fully match the true relationship, but they’re more stable across different samples. Empirically driven models usually have lower bias and higher variance since they can fit the data more closely, but that also makes them more sensitive to noise. So the trade-off is that mechanistic models may underfit but be consistent, while empirically driven models can capture more complexity but risk overfitting.
:::

### Question 5 

Explain the difference between a regression and classification machine learning (ML) problems.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Stratifying by the outcome variable makes sure each split has a similar distribution of the target as the full dataset. This is especially important when the classes are imbalanced, so you don’t end up with a training or validation set that has too many or too few of one class. We use it because it leads to more reliable model evaluation. Without stratification, performance metrics like RMSE or accuracy could be misleading just due to an uneven split rather than the model itself.
:::

### Question 6 

When splitting the data, why is it useful to stratify by the outcome/target variable? 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

### Question 7 

Briefly describe how v-fold cross validation with repeats is used to estimate test RMSE. Also provide an explanation of why we use it. 

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

In v-fold cross validation, we split the training data into v equal parts, train the model on v-1 folds, and evaluate it on the remaining fold. This gets repeated so each fold serves as the validation set once, and we average the RMSE across all folds. When we add repeats, we reshuffle the data and do the whole process multiple times, then average across all runs to get a more stable estimate.

We use this because it gives a better approximation of test RMSE than a single split. It reduces the chance that our results depend on one lucky (or unlucky) split and helps us get a more reliable sense of how the model will perform on new data.
:::

### Question 8

When might we use a bootstrap resampling procedure instead of v-fold cross validation to estimate test RMSE?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

You’d usually use bootstrap instead of v-fold CV when your dataset is pretty small or when you want a better sense of the variability of your model’s performance. Bootstrap resampling lets you reuse observations more flexibly (since samples are drawn with replacement), which can give more stable estimates of RMSE in low-data settings. It’s also helpful if you care about getting confidence intervals around your performance metric, not just a single estimate.
:::

### Question 9 

Briefly describe model tuning and why we use it.

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Model tuning is the process of adjusting a model’s hyperparameters (like number of neighbors in KNN or tree depth) to find the combination that gives the best performance. We use it because the default settings usually aren’t optimal, and small changes can noticeably improve how well the model predicts on new data. It helps us get the best version of a model without overfitting.
:::

### Question 10 

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a regression ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

RMSE and MAE

:::

What are two common performance metrics when dealing with a classification ML problem?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

Accuracy and ROC AUC
:::

### Question 11

Classify each question/problem below as either prediction or inferential. Explain your reasoning for each.

A company with a subscription based service (for example Netflix, Disney+, New York Times, etc) has data concerning customer interactions with a them, including features like the number of customer service calls, quality of service calls, subscription length, engagement with the service, discounted service, etc. They are interested in two questions:

1. Does a high number of service calls impact a customer's likelihood of churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::

2. How likely is it that a customer will churn in the next month (leave the company/drop the service)?

::: {.callout-tip icon="false"}
## Solution

YOUR SOLUTION HERE

:::
